title: [Bug]: Using podman kube play and hostPort, ports bound to localhost are not accessible
body:
### Issue Description

When deploying a Pod with `podman kube play` and using hostPort to make a port accessible on the host machine, the port is not accessible if it is bound to `localhost` inside the container. It is accessible only if bound to address `0.0.0.0`.

Is this by design? Do we need to always bind to 0.0.0.0 if we need to access the port? Or is there another way to access the port from the host, using `podman kube play`?

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue

```
$ cat pod-localhost.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: my-app
spec:
  containers:
  - image: hashicorp/http-echo
    args:
    - -listen=localhost:5678
    - -text=pong
    name: main
    ports:
    - containerPort: 5678
      hostPort: 10000

$ podman kube play pod-localhost.yaml

$ curl localhost:10000
curl: (56) Recv failure: Connection reset by peer
```





### Describe the results you received

As described above

### Describe the results you expected

I expect to get a response.

Note that it works with listening on 0.0.0.0:

```
$ podman pod stop my-app 
$ podman pod rm my-app 


$ cat pod-all.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: my-app
spec:
  containers:
  - image: hashicorp/http-echo
    args:
    - -listen=0.0.0.0:5678
    - -text=pong
    name: main
    ports:
    - containerPort: 5678
      hostPort: 10000

$ podman kube play pod-all.yaml

$ curl localhost:10000
pong
```

Also note that the port can be forwarded when the container is running on Kubernetes:

```
$ cat kpod-localhost.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: my-app
spec:
  containers:
  - image: hashicorp/http-echo
    args:
    - -listen=localhost:5678
    - -text=pong
    name: main
    ports:
    - containerPort: 5678


$ kubectl apply -f kpod-localhost.yaml 

$ kubectl port-forward pod/my-app 5678
```

```
$ curl localhost:5678
pong
```

### Additional information

comments.body:
Yes this is expected. Packages are always send to the "eth0" interface in the container.
It is impossible to send a packet to localhost from another network namespace.
